title: Depletion-mode N-polar AlN-based high electron mobility transistors with improved on/off ratios
authors: Xu Yang, Sheng Zhang, Ke Wei, Xinhua Wang, Xinyu Liu, Itsuki Furuhashi, Markus Pristovsek
abstract: We report N-polar AlN-based high-electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) with a GaN channel thickness of 5.2 nm on N-polar AlN on sapphire. The threshold voltage is around -2.4 to -3.0 V with saturation currents over 240 mA/mm and on/off ratios as high as 10,000, much higher than previously reported N-polar AlN-based HEMTs. The high on/off ratio is attributed to the use of an abrupt AlN/GaN heterostructure with a dedicated AlN transition layer, together with improved gate leakage. The high frequency properties as well as the on-resistance of ~20 Ohm mm are all limited by the 2000 Ohm/square sheet resistance of the channel layer.
summary: The researchers developed N-polar AlN-based transistors with significantly improved on/off ratios by replacing AlGaN layers with an abrupt AlN/GaN interface. This structural change reduces gate leakage and prevents a secondary electron gas from acting as a leakage path. The resulting devices show high saturation currents and improved power efficiency for high-frequency applications.
main_problem: Improving the performance of N-polar AlN-based high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) by addressing low on/off ratios, high gate leakage, and impurity incorporation.
main_result: Achieved an on/off ratio as high as 10,000 and saturation currents over 240 mA/mm by using an abrupt AlN/GaN heterostructure with a dedicated AlN transition layer.
method: Metal-organic vapor phase epitaxy (MOVPE) was used to grow N-polar AlN on sapphire, utilizing a temperature and NH3 flow ramping technique to optimize the transition layer and reduce impurities.
model_or_system: N-polar AlN-based HEMTs featuring a 5.2 nm GaN channel and an abrupt AlN/GaN heterostructure grown on (000-1) sapphire substrates.
key_observables: Threshold voltage (Vth), saturation current (Isat), on/off ratio, gate leakage current, transconductance (gm), and RF cutoff frequencies (fT, fMAX).
important_parameters: GaN channel thickness of 5.2 nm, sheet resistance of 2000 Ohm/square, and RMS surface roughness below 0.5 nm.
main_assumptions: The performance is limited by the high sheet resistance resulting from dislocation density inherent in hetero-epitaxial growth on sapphire.
figures_summary: Figure 1a shows low interface roughness; Figure 2d displays transfer curves and subthreshold slope; Figure 4 presents measured RF cutoff frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the device architecture, details the MOVPE growth optimization and impurity control, presents structural characterization (XRD, AFM, SIMS), reports electrical and RF performance metrics, and discusses limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) XRD 2Θ −ω measurement around 0002 AlN (blue) and simulation (red dashed) of coherent 5.2 nm GaN (red dashed). Inset: Surface morphology of the GaN channel, exhibiting the RMS surface roughness of 0.22 nm, scale bar: 1 µm. (b) XRD reciprocal space map around the AlN 10¯15 re- flection, confirming a fully strained GaN channel without any AlGaN intermixing. (c) SIMS of a comparable sample with double AlN transition layer growth times (for higher resolu- tion). The top panel shows the variation in NH3 flow as V/III ratio and growth temperature.

FIG. 2. (a) Cross-sectional representation of a processed HEMT. (b) Output characteristic for a 50 µm × 2.4 µm HEMT device. Gate-to-Source voltage VGS changed from -6 to 2 V in the step of 1 V. Corresponding linear (c) and semi-logarithmic (d) plots of transfer curves and gate leakage curves measured at Vds = 6 V.

FIG. 3. a) band diagram (left axis) with the states of the AlN/AlGaN 2DEG (red) and the AlGaN/GaN 2DEG (blue). Right axis is the sheet electron concentration. b) calculated capacitance from a) indications two thresholds for an AlGaN intermediate layer.
FIG. 4. Measured cutoff frequencies of a HEMT with LG = 250 nm in this study, showing highest fT = 11.3 GHz and fMAX = 27 GHz.
